subject: mathematics
chapter_title: I'm Up and Down, and Round and Round

sections.title: Definitions
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to infer the method Amina suggested for locating the centre of a circular piece of paper.

sections.title: Symmetries of a Circle
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to identify the rotational and reflection symmetries of a square, regular pentagon, and regular hexagon, the length of the longest and smallest chord in a circle of given radius, and the locus of points equidistant from two given points.

sections.title: How Many Circles?
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to reason about how many circles pass through two points, the radii of the smallest and largest such circles, how curvature changes along the perpendicular bisector, and how many squares can be drawn with two given points on the boundary or as corners.

sections.title: Chords and the Angles They Subtend
Midpoints and Perpendicular Bisectors of Chords
Distance of Chords from the Centre
Which of the two unequal chords is farther from the centre?
Angle subtended by an arc at a point on the circle outside the arc

prose_summary: Section 5.1 defines a circle as the locus of points in a plane equidistant from a fixed centre, and introduces radius, chord, and diameter, building on the Grade 7 observation that all points on a circle are equidistant from its centre. Section 5.2 explains and justifies the complete rotational symmetry of a circle about its centre and the reflection symmetry of a circle across any diameter, using a paper-folding activity to show that every diameter is a line of reflection symmetry. Section 5.3 derives that infinitely many circles pass through two given points A and B, that the centres of all such circles lie on the perpendicular bisector of AB, and proves Theorem 1 that a unique circle (the circumcircle) passes through three non-collinear points, introducing circumcentre and circumcircle and locating the circumcentre relative to acute, obtuse, and right-angled triangles. Section 5.4 proves Theorem 2 (equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre) and Theorem 3 (chords subtending equal angles at the centre are equal) using SSS and SAS triangle congruence respectively, building the converse relationship between chord length and central angle. Section 5.5 proves Theorem 4 (the line joining the centre to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord) via an isosceles-triangle congruence argument, and states Theorem 5 (the perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects it) as the converse, established through the exercise set. Section 5.6 proves Theorem 6 (chords of equal length are equidistant from the centre) by two independent congruence arguments and Theorem 7 (chords equidistant from the centre are equal in length) via RHS congruence, using a paper-folding and tracing-paper activity to motivate the result before the formal proof. Section 5.6.1 proves Theorem 8 (of two unequal chords, the longer chord lies closer to the centre) using the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem, and notes as a comment that the diameter is the chord nearest the centre while a chord shrinking to a point sits at distance equal to the radius. Section 5.7 defines arc, major arc, minor arc, and the angle subtended by an arc at the centre, distinguishing the angle swept via the minor arc from that swept via the major arc. Section 5.7.1 proves Theorem 9 (the angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle it subtends at any point on the circle outside the arc) by splitting the argument into two cases using the exterior-angle property of isosceles triangles, derives the corollary that the angle subtended by a diameter at any point on the circle is 90 degrees, and establishes that angles subtended by the same arc at different points outside it are all equal. Section 5.8 proves Theorem 10 (if a segment AB subtends equal angles at two points C, D on the same side of AB, then A, B, C, D are concyclic) by contradiction, defines cyclic quadrilateral, proves Theorem 11 (opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180 degrees) using the central-angle relationship from Theorem 9, and proves Theorem 12, the converse of Theorem 11, also by contradiction.

enumerated_exercises.description: Draw triangle ABC with AB = 5 cm, angle A = 70 degrees, angle B = 60 degrees; draw its circumcircle and state whether the centre lies inside or outside the triangle.
Draw triangle ABC with AB = 5 cm, angle A = 100 degrees, AC = 4 cm; draw its circumcircle and state whether the centre lies inside or outside the triangle.
Draw triangle ABC with AB = 6 cm, BC = 7 cm, CA = 7 cm; draw the circumcircle, mark circumcentre O, and measure OA, OB, OC.
Find the least possible radius of a circle through two given points A and B.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Think, Draw and Infer Q1, p.98
enumerated_exercises.description: For three collinear points A, B, C, investigate whether a point P exists with PA = PB = PC; examine the perpendicular bisectors of AB and BC, show they are parallel, and decide whether a circle can pass through collinear points or whether a line can cut a circle in three points.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Think, Draw and Infer Q2, p.98
enumerated_exercises.description: Given the circumcircle of triangle ABC, determine whether other triangles congruent to ABC can share the same circumcircle.
Explain why the converse of Theorem 4 is true: the perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.
For isosceles triangle ABC inscribed in a circle with AB = AC, show that the altitude from A to BC passes through the centre.
Two parallel chords of lengths 6 cm and 8 cm lie on opposite sides of the centre of a circle of radius 5 cm; find the distance between the midpoints of the chords.
Show that the triangle formed by a chord and the centre of the circle is isosceles.
Show that two such isosceles triangles with equal base length are congruent to each other.
Use the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem to show why Theorem 6 must be true.
Given CE perpendicular to AB, CH perpendicular to GH, and CE = CH, show that AB = GF.
Solve the previous question using the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem.
Find the length of a chord of a circle with radius 7 cm and perpendicular distance 6 cm from the centre.
Explain why the chord length formula 2 times square root of (r squared minus d squared) holds for perpendicular distance d and radius r.
Measure the angles subtended by arc AKB and arc CLD at centre O and state whether each is a minor or major arc.
In a circle with centre O, the central angle AOB is 60 degrees and the radius is 12 cm; find the length of chord AB.

For two points A, B on a circle with centre O, investigate whether points X, Y on the same side of AB can subtend different angles AXB and AYB, whether equal subtended angles imply X and Y are on the same side, and whether the circle through A, B, X also passes through Y when angles are equal.
Find x in the given figure where a cyclic-type angle configuration has one angle of 100 degrees.
A cyclic quadrilateral has angles A = 80 degrees, B = 110 degrees, C = 100 degrees, D = 70 degrees; determine whether such a quadrilateral can be drawn and explain why.
In a circle, a chord is 5 cm from the centre; if the radius is 13 cm, find the chord length.
An arc subtends 70 degrees at the centre; find the angle the arc subtends at a point on the circle.
The diameter of a circle is 26 cm and a chord of length 24 cm is drawn; find the distance from the centre to the chord.
A circle of radius 15 cm has a chord at distance 9 cm from the centre; find the chord length.
Prove that the perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre of the circle.
AB is a diameter and C is on the circumference; find the measure of angle ACB and explain the reasoning.
ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral with angle A = 75 degrees; find angle C, and with angle B = 110 degrees, find angle D.
Quadrilateral PQRS is inscribed in a circle with angle P = (2x+10) degrees and angle R = (3x-20) degrees; find x and the measures of angle P and angle R.
A chord of length 16 cm is 6 cm from the centre; find the radius of the circle.
A cyclic quadrilateral has sides 5, 5, 12, 12 units; find its area.
Two parallel chords of lengths 10 cm and 24 cm lie on the same side of the centre, 7 cm apart; find the radius of the circle.
Quadrilateral MNOP is inscribed in a circle with MN a diameter; state and explain what can be said about angles MOP and MNP.
For cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, explain why the exterior angle at any vertex equals the interior opposite angle.
Use the given figure to justify that the angle in a semicircle is 90 degrees.
Use the given figure to justify that the sum of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180 degrees.
